s1_b1: Yousef built a spring launcher. Press a ball onto the spring, release, and it should rise and tap a bell.
s1_b2: One goal. The ball just reaches the bell — taps it — and no higher.
s1_b3: He presses the spring a little. The ball pops up and falls short, well below the bell.
s1_b4: So he presses it down hard. The ball flies way past the bell and keeps going.
s1_b5: He eases off a little — and it still overshoots, by more than the easing should explain.
s1_b6: A heavier ball with the same press barely clears his hand. Same spring, completely different height.
s1_b7: He wasn't short of presses. He was short of an account — where the squeeze goes, and what it has to become.
s1_b8: The squeeze stores energy in the spring. Let go, and it becomes the ball's speed.
s1_b9: And the speed buys height — trading itself away as the ball climbs, until none is left and it stops at the top.
s1_b10: Spring energy becomes motion becomes height. The same total, just changing form — and a heavier ball turns the same total into less height.
s1_b11: Before he presses again — he builds it where he can see the energy move.
s1_b12: Masses and Springs. He sets the spring stiffness and the mass. Turns the energy bars on.
s1_b13: Here is the real job. Not releasing the spring. Predicting — before he does.
s1_b14: How far must he compress it so the stored energy becomes exactly the height of the bell — for this ball's mass?
s1_b15: Then he releases. And he watches the bars hand energy along, not the ball.
s1_b16: After — he explains the gap. Overshot? Too much stored. Fell short? Too little. The bars name the error.
s1_b17: He adjusts the compression. Predicts again. Three tries. That's all he gets.
s1_b18: His first prediction is wrong. That is not the problem. That is the point. Each miss tells him which form he mismeasured.
s1_b19: Now he sets the real launcher. Once. The ball rises, taps the bell, and falls back.
s1_b20: The bell was never the homework. This is: in one sentence, your own words — trace one unit of energy from the squeeze to the top.
s1_b21: And if this felt familiar — it should. Do you remember the drawn bow that didn't move, holding energy in its bend?
s1_b22: And the book on the high shelf, holding energy it hadn't spent? And the cart that turned a push into pure speed?
s1_b23: And the pendulum that never lost a thing — only kept trading it, back and forth?
s1_b24: This machine was all of them at once. That is the concept. Now you know which videos to go back to.
